success_surface: Cross-reference lookup task requiring data extraction from one column, pattern matching against a reference range, and population of result values in target cells
effective_behaviors: Conducted progressive exploration before final solution generation
Built in-memory lookup dictionary from reference columns before applying transformations
Used string splitting with delimiters to extract embedded data patterns
Detected data boundaries dynamically using sheet metadata rather than hardcoding ranges
Verified output integrity by reloading saved workbook after writes
Added null/value existence checks before processing each cell
evidence: First two interactions used read-only mode to explore sheet structure and identify column positions
Code created code_to_month dictionary iterating through reference rows before modifying target cells
Used str(a_value).split('_') to extract month_part from format like YYYY_MM_*
Used range(2, min(ws.max_row + 1, 20)) instead of fixed row numbers
Final code included wb_verify = load_workbook(output_path) block to print verification data
Applied if code_cell and month_cell condition before adding to dictionary

memory_items.title: Progressive Data Exploration
memory_items.lesson: Before solving complex cross-reference tasks, use initial read-only passes to map out relevant columns, data formats, and reference ranges
memory_items.generalization: When task involves multi-step logic or unfamiliar worksheet structure, separate analysis phase from execution phase; gather schema information first without making modifications

memory_items.title: In-Memory Reference Mapping
memory_items.lesson: For lookups spanning different column pairs, build a Python dictionary from source-to-target column mappings before iterating over destination cells
memory_items.generalization: Avoid repeated row-by-row searches; cache reference relationships in memory structures (dicts, sets) during initialization, then apply during transformation loop

memory_items.title: Pattern Extraction from Formatted Strings
memory_items.lesson: Use string delimiter splitting when embedded values follow consistent formatting patterns within cell text
memory_items.generalization: When target values are embedded in composite strings (date codes, concatenated fields), identify delimiters and extract specific positional components rather than using regex unless necessary

memory_items.title: Dynamic Range Detection
memory_items.lesson: Query workbook properties like max_row or iter_rows() to determine actual data bounds instead of assuming fixed row counts
memory_items.generalization: Adapt iteration loops to worksheet metadata for robustness across files with varying record counts; always add upper bound guards to prevent index errors

memory_items.title: Output Validation Protocol
memory_items.lesson: After saving modifications, reload the output file to confirm changes persisted as expected before considering task complete
memory_items.generalization: Include verification step that loads saved output and spot-checks critical cells to catch serialization errors early in development cycles

memory_items.title: Null Safety in Cell Operations
memory_items.lesson: Check for None or empty values before processing any cell content to avoid runtime exceptions
memory_items.generalization: Apply defensive programming for all cell reads: validate presence and type before splitting, converting, or using as lookup keys

edits.content:
## Trace-Derived Lessons

### 1. Progressive Exploration Strategy
Before executing complex transformations, perform read-only exploratory passes to map worksheet structure, identify reference ranges, and understand data formats. Separate discovery phase from execution phase to avoid premature commits.

### 2. Lookup Table Pre-Caching
For cross-column reference tasks where target values depend on matching values elsewhere, build in-memory dictionaries from source-to-result column mappings during initialization. Apply cached mappings during iteration to avoid repeated searches.

### 3. Pattern-Based String Extraction
When cell values contain embedded data following consistent delimiters (underscores, dashes, etc.), extract components via string splitting at known positions rather than using heavy regex patterns unless complexity warrants it.

### 4. Metadata-Driven Range Detection
Always query worksheet metadata (max_row, has_data, etc.) to determine iteration bounds dynamically. Never assume fixed row counts; add ceiling guards to loops for files with varying record lengths.

### 5. Post-Save Verification Routine
After writing modifications, reload the output workbook to verify critical cells received expected values. This catches serialization issues and confirms persistence before task completion.

### 6. Defensive Null Handling
Validate cell value existence before applying string operations or using as lookup keys. Wrap processing in conditional checks to gracefully skip empty or corrupted rows.
